categories.label: Church & Wardens
encounters: Warden checkpoint — two bored officers, checking papers. Haven't seen the notice yet.
Warden checkpoint — notice is posted. They're checking faces now.
Warden checkpoint — understaffed, one officer doing the work of three. Distracted. Easy to talk past.
Warden patrol on horseback — three riders moving fast down the road in the opposite direction. Not stopping. Headed somewhere specific.
Warden licensing audit at a roadside inn. Every instrument in the building is being catalogued. The innkeeper is furious.
Approved-songs ceremony in a field. A Conservatory officer leads thirty farmers through a hymn. The crops look terrible.
A freshly painted Conservatory waypost. The paint is still wet. Someone has added the party's descriptions underneath the standard notices.
Travelling Conservatory cleric offering free blessings and rations from a cart. Genuine. Knows nothing. The rations are real and the people need them.
A measurement team — two Church technicians with new-model devices, taking readings at a roadside marker stone. They seem confused by their own results.
Burned-out Warden station. Recent. No bodies. Nobody in the nearest town wants to talk about it.
A Confirmed officer (not one of the named three) travelling alone. Full armor, saying nothing, moving with purpose. Headed somewhere the party might also be going.
Church-sponsored roadwork crew — repairing a bridge. The foreman mentions supply shortages have doubled the timeline.
A wanted notice for the party, but the descriptions are wrong. Someone reported them inaccurately. For now.
Two Wardens arguing about whether to arrest a farmer playing an unapproved song to his failing crop. One thinks it's harmless. One thinks it's the law.
A Church courier overtakes the party on the road. Sealed documents. Moving fast. Doesn't stop.
A village where the approved-songs ceremony has been cancelled. The officer assigned to lead it hasn't shown up in two weeks.
A group of displaced Pale Hand clergy walking the road with their belongings. Quiet. Dignified. Going somewhere that isn't clear.
Conservatory recruiters at a crossroads, signing up young musicians for the "Restoration Program." Promising meals, housing, purpose.
A roadside shrine to all four gods. Someone has recently scratched out one of the four symbols. The scratch marks are fresh.
A Warden who recognises the party but pretends not to. Waves them through. Says nothing. Never explained.

categories.label: Resonant Activity
encounters: A broken-string symbol scratched into a milestone. Recent.
A travelling merchant who uses Resonant hand-signs without realising the party can see. Selling totally normal goods. The signs are for someone else down the road.
An abandoned campsite with a Resonant measuring device hidden under a rock. Still functional. Left in a hurry.
Graffiti on a Church waypost — "THE READINGS ARE WRONG" in careful letters. Already half-scrubbed.
A busker playing an unapproved song in a market town. Drawing a crowd. Two Wardens approaching from the far side of the square.
A dead drop that isn't for the party — a sealed tube in a hollow tree, visible from the road. Contains information about Church shipping schedules.
A Resonant cell member on the run — wounded, paranoid, won't trust the party without proof. Has information about a nearby cultivation site if they can win them over.
A pamphlet stuck to a tree. Resonant propaganda. Badly written but factually accurate.
Evidence of a Resonant sabotage — a Church supply wagon with cut axles, cargo scattered. The supplies are just rations and measurement equipment.
A tavern where the innkeeper plays a very old song after closing. Pre-Silence. Anyone with musical training can tell the tuning is different from modern approved songs.

categories.label: Echo & Silence Effects
encounters: A stretch of road where all sound drops to half volume. Lasts about a mile. Animals avoid it.
A field of wildflowers that are all the same colour — they shouldn't be. Bees won't land on them.
A creek where the water sounds like it's humming. Not a tune — a drone. AV's Metrognome ticks irregularly near it.
A farmstead where the family speaks in monotone. Not hostile, not sad — flat. They've been this way for weeks. The crops are grey.
A crossroads where an echo repeats words that weren't spoken. Says something different each time the party passes.
A tree split down the middle, still alive on both sides. The split hums when the wind passes through it. Locals leave offerings.
A flock of birds flying in perfect geometric patterns. Unnaturally perfect. They don't break formation.
A stretch of road where the party's instruments won't stay in tune. Constant micro-drift. Maddening for musicians.
A village well that produces water tasting of copper since last month. The Church measurement team said everything was fine.
A sleeping Hollow — small, dog-sized, curled up in a ditch like roadkill. Not dead. Not yet dangerous. Visibly made of compressed sound given wrong shape.

categories.label: The Road
encounters: A travelling merchant with an overloaded cart. Sells common supplies at fair prices. Gossips freely — knows which towns have checkpoints and which don't.
A broken wagon blocking the road. The family needs help with the wheel. Genuine — no ambush, just bad luck.
A crossroads inn with a surprisingly good house band. The crowd is large for a place this small. Something about the music here draws people.
A bridge troll. Literally. Wants a toll of one song per person. Judges harshly but fairly.
A funeral procession on the road. A Pale Hand priest leads it. The family asked specifically for Maelon's rites, not the Church's.
A travelling Dunwall Co. distributor — Voice tier, selling sunscreen and laser hair removal rocks from a handcart. Recognises the party's product. Excited. Wants tips.
Rain. Three days of it. The road becomes mud. Movement halves. Morale tested.
A roadside inn where the party is recognised — not as fugitives, but as musicians. Someone saw them at Trill-fest. Wants an autograph. Loudly.
A merchant caravan with armed guards. Headed the opposite direction. The guards are tense — they've heard about bandit activity ahead. It's actually Resonant activity.
A group of pilgrims heading to Solmara for a Church festival. Friendly, talkative, absolutely certain the approved-songs program is working.
A fork in the road with no signpost. One path is maintained. The other is overgrown but shorter. The overgrown path passes near something interesting.
A competitor band on the road — nobody famous, just working musicians trying to get to the next gig. Friendly rivalry or useful allies.
A livestock drive blocking the road for an hour. The ranchers apologise. Their animals look thin.
An abandoned coaching inn. Recently vacated. Food still on the table. No signs of violence — everyone just left.
A river crossing where the ferry operator charges double because "the roads aren't safe." Won't explain further.
A travelling tinker who repairs instruments. Good work, fair prices. Mentions that new instruments from the Conservatory have started sounding "samey" to her ear.
A stretch of old-growth forest where the canopy is thick enough to block scrying. The party doesn't know this yet. The Metrognome settles into a calm, steady tick.
A milestone marking distance to two cities. One distance is wrong — recently re-chiseled. Someone is misdirecting traffic away from something.
Dunwall Co. recruitment poster nailed to a tree. Albus's face. "BE YOUR OWN MAESTRO." The irony is unbearable.
A campsite used by someone who left in a hurry. Cold fire. Scattered gear. A journal page blowing in the wind — a Pale Hand sister's diary entry about being reassigned from her hospice.

categories.label: Threats
encounters: Bandits. Three. Desperate, not professional. They'll take food over gold. The roads are getting worse.
A Hollow — small, fast, sounds like a violin string wound too tight. Came from somewhere nearby. There shouldn't be one this far from a known site.
Wolves. Normal wolves. The world still has normal problems.
A rockslide blocks the road. Natural, not sabotage. Costs half a day to clear or find a way around.
A bounty hunter — solo, professional, has the party's descriptions. Not Church-affiliated. Someone is paying privately.
An ambush by Church-adjacent thugs — not Wardens, not Confirmed. Hired muscle. Deniable. They have a list of questions about where the party has been.
A Hollow manifestation at a roadside shrine — a shade that looks like a musician, playing a song that isn't there. Not hostile unless approached. Deeply sad.
A bridge washed out. Recent storm. The detour adds a day and passes through a town with a checkpoint.
A pack of wild dogs that have been following the party since the last town. Not attacking. Just following. One of them is humming.
A tree across the road — cut, not fallen. Someone put it there. The forest on both sides is very quiet.
Fog. Thick, sudden, wrong. Sound carries strangely in it — conversations from a mile away arrive clearly, the party's own voices don't carry ten feet.
A sinkhole opens ahead on the road. Below: old stone. Not natural. Possibly a buried structure. Investigating costs time the party may not have.
A Church patrol specifically looking for the party. Six Wardens, one officer. They have accurate descriptions and orders to detain.
Night encounter — something large moves through the camp without disturbing anything. Tracks in the morning are musical notation pressed into the dirt.
A diseased animal blocks the road — aggressive, frothing, not right. Echo-sick, not rabid. Killing it feels wrong. Leaving it feels worse.

categories.label: People & Opportunities
encounters: A Pale Hand hospice — small, underfunded, doing real work. The sister running it mentions Church officials visited last week asking about "transitioning services."
A farmer who'll trade shelter for a performance. Doesn't care about approved songs. "Play something real. The crops need it."
A retired Warden living off the road. Drinks too much. Will talk about how the service has changed in the last decade if prompted.
A child on the road alone. Lost, not endangered — their village is nearby. Walking them home reveals a community that's quietly ignoring Church mandates.
A Pale Hand priest performing last rites in an open field. The body is a young person. No cause of death is obvious. The priest says "another one" quietly.
A noble's carriage breaks down near the party. The noble is heading to Bellanthorpe and offers passage in exchange for help — skipping a checkpoint in the process.
A university student researching folklore. Has transcribed old songs. Doesn't understand why the melodies she's collected don't match the approved versions. Hasn't made the connection.
A mapmaker who will trade a regional map for news of the road ahead. The map is accurate and marks water sources, shelters, and "places that feel strange."
A group of Echo-sick travellers being moved by Church escorts to an undisclosed facility. The escorts are polite and firm. The travellers look vacant.
A Pale Hand lay-sister heading to Highmist. Travelling alone, unbothered. Mentions the hospice there is "changing management."
A bard from out of region who has never heard of approved songs. Baffled by the concept. Plays whatever she wants. Hasn't been stopped yet.

An old soldier who fought in a border conflict decades ago. Mentions that the songs his unit sang actually kept them alive — wounds closed faster, morale held. "They don't let you sing those ones anymore."
A merchant who deals in pre-Silence instruments. Expensive, secretive, moves fast. Has exactly one item the party might want.
A town crier announcing new Church policies — increased measurement frequency, expanded approved-song lists, "voluntary" compliance reporting. The crowd listens silently.
A wandering painter capturing landscapes. Has accidentally painted Echo effects into several pieces — the wrongness is visible in the art even when the eye slides off it in person.

A Dunwall Co. Conductor-tier distributor running a regional operation. Wants to know why the party's name is on a wanted notice. Could become a problem or an asset depending on how the party handles it.
A healer (not Church-affiliated) treating Echo-sick patients with music. It's working, slowly. She doesn't know why and doesn't care — results matter.
Two Church officials arguing at a crossroads inn. One is insisting measurements are wrong. The other is insisting they follow protocol. The first one has an old-model device.
A town preparing for a festival that technically requires Warden approval. They haven't applied. Nobody mentions this. The party is invited to play.
A Resonant sympathiser who runs a waystation. Offers shelter, no questions. The broken-string mark is hidden but findable.

categories.label: Scrying & Surveillance
encounters: The Metrognome ticks wrong for exactly thirty seconds. Then stops. Then resumes normally. Someone was listening.
Every stringed instrument in the party's possession hums the same note simultaneously. Brief. Unsettling. A passing farmer doesn't notice.
The party hears their own conversation repeated back to them from a nearby tree — ten seconds delayed, word for word. Then silence.
AV's Metrognome goes completely still for a full minute. No ticks at all. When it resumes, it's slightly faster than before, as if catching up.
A note the party didn't play hangs in the air after a performance. Visible as a faint shimmer. Fades slowly. Someone was watching that one.
